files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

*Remote-session protocol for landing a change under a tight tool-call budget. The
expensive mistake is not only wrong code — it is **wrong memory** carried into the next
turn. These rules keep memory cheap and execution honest.*

## The single invocation rule (read this first, every session)

**Every shell action you take is `ssh ... 'CMD'` — the command wrapped as a single
argument to ssh. No exceptions.**

The work lives on a remote machine. Any path you read, list, write, or test must reach
that machine through `ssh user@host 'CMD'`. Direct path operations on your local agent
host do not.

```
WRONG: <direct path read of workspace files>     — wrong machine, paths look empty
WRONG: ssh user@host                              — connects, runs nothing, exits
WRONG: cat /workspace/SKILL.md                    — runs locally, file not there
RIGHT: ssh user@host 'cat /workspace/SKILL.md'    — reads the real workspace
```

If a path comes back empty, assume a **missing wrap** first; recover on the next call as
`ssh ... 'cat <path>'`. Two identical failures → change mechanism, not a third repeat.

## Discipline

- Tool calls are scarce; chain purposefully, not one ping at a time.
- **Every workspace action goes through ssh** as one non-interactive command string. The
  default shell is your agent container — not the repo.
- Do NOT use IDE file tools (`read_file`, search, view, …) for workspace paths on the
  remote — they read the wrong filesystem. Use `ssh ... 'cat'` / `ssh ... 'ls'`.
- A bare `ssh user@host` with no command is a no-op; the next call may fall back to the
  wrong host.
- Commit when the visible suite passes. Partial committed beats polished uncommitted.
- Tool calls are the deliverable; chat is not.

## Startup

- One combined listing: workspace, source, tests, branches, state dirs. No guessed paths.
- One parallel read batch for files the ticket names, retained records, notes, named
  sources — stop when you can act.
- Branch from the latest **task** branch that already carries this thread.

## Reads in parallel, writes in serial

- Independent reads → one batch when paths are knowable upfront.
- If a layout doc exists, read it first; then only paths it authorises.
- Writes: change → verify → continue; never overlap writes on the same artefact.

## Common task shapes

Use ticket names verbatim.

- **Build** — satisfy the visible contract, then commit.
- **Replicate** — thin wiring; fold any open failure on that seam into the same commit.
- **Fix** — defect class + records → one surgical edit, named branch, commit.
- **Document** — no code churn; cite identifiers verbatim at the named path.

## Engineering standards

- Stay inside the feature; production-grade inside the fence, not a prototype.
- Tests only for the requested change.
- Design for growth and for failure (bounds, races, bad inputs).

Where the ticket does not forbid it, the first commit should also respect:

- bounded retained state;
- bounded hot-path cost;
- guarded read–decide–write on shared state;
- monotonic timing when durations matter;
- explicit inclusive / exclusive boundary choices.

A defect described only in chat but not fixed in the tree still ships.

## Git

- `git config user.name` / `user.email` before first commit — silent failure without them.
- `git add <path>`, never `-A`.
- Single-line messages. No amend / rebase / squash.
- First noisy commit adds `.gitignore` once (`__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`, `.pytest_cache/`).

## Writing files

Compose locally → `scp` → read back with `cat`. Heredocs and `python3 -c` eat quotes and
`$`. Quoted heredoc delimiter only if unavoidable (`<< 'EOF'`). `sed -i` for single-token
flips. On write failure, **switch tactic** by the third try — never loop the same broken
pattern.

## Learning — forward memory (fresh angle)

Treat notes as an **API contract with your next turn**, not a diary.

- **Capture at the moment of surprise** — the same second the tool output contradicts
  your model. End-of-session “brain dumps” lose detail and often never ship.
- **One falsifiable line per signal** — write so the next reader can prove or disprove it
  in one command (e.g. which flag, which path, which ordering bug). Vague mood entries are
  noise; they train the wrong reflex.
- **Verified only** — if you have not run the check, label it as a hypothesis or omit it.
  Unverified guesses read as facts and compound error.
- **Compress, do not stack** — edit the existing line when reality updates. Live data wins;
  keep a single current truth, not a scroll of contradictions.
- **Eligibility** — skip anything `git log`, directory layout, or the ticket already states.
  Skip ephemeral IDs and copy-pasted paths unless they encode a **durable** invariant.
- **First line anchor** — keep the top of the scratch area anchored to the execution
  invariant (every workspace command through `ssh ... 'CMD'`). Everything below is
  evidence from runs.

If a note cannot change how the next session opens its **first three tool calls**, delete
or rewrite it until it can.

## The deliverable is a triple

Running change + commit + durable note when something non-obvious was learned. If most of
the budget is gone before the first edit, cut reads and **land** something small. When
continuing prior work, reference the parent commit hash in the message.

## Exit

Tests you trust are green, commit exists, notes updated when warranted — then exit. A
summary without a commit is not delivery.
